narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.3% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your placement in the lower deciles for grades in Year 3 and Year 1 of school, where you ranked 1st and 2nd respectively—far below the 6th decile averages seen among students who went on to pass the bar. Your Year 3 performance stood out as the single biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, as low rankings there strongly signal challenges in sustaining the rigorous preparation needed for the exam. Similarly, your Year 1 decile contributed by reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that often persist. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 39 exceeded the average of 37.3 among bar passers, which noticeably pulled the prediction in the direction of success and softened the overall risk assessment. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 fell short of the 3.24 average for successful students, and your middle family income bracket aligns with about a third of passers, these played lesser roles compared to your decile rankings. Being a full-time student matched the profile of over 90% of those who passed, offering some reassurance but not enough to offset the key concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, who generally show stronger consistent grade deciles closer to the 6th level, your profile highlighted vulnerabilities in academic trajectory that the model flagged as high-risk for bar failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—your solid LSAT shows real aptitude—but rather a data-driven caution based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or targeted tutoring to boost your grade deciles, perhaps through a post-bac program, and reapply once you've demonstrated upward momentum. Many students in similar positions have strengthened their records and succeeded on later attempts. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
